data.title: Chart Of The Day: Who's Winning, Who's Losing As We Close Out Q3
data.description:
By Mike Larson

We’re in the waning days of the third quarter – and that means the year-end sprint is about to begin. So, which S&P 500 Index (SPX) sectors are winning? Which are losing? And what does that say about the market's "character?"

Take a look at the MoneyShow Chart of the Day. It shows the performance of the 11 S&P 500 sector SPDR funds from the start of the quarter through Friday.

Q3 Sector Report Card: Who’s Winning, Who’s Losing

You can see that all in all, it has been a pretty solid quarter. Only three of 11 sector ETFs – those targeting health care, materials, and consumer staples – are trading in the red in the waning days of Q3. Plus, the worst loss is just 5% for the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLP).

Of the winners, only one is up double digits. It’s the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK), with a gain of 11%. But three other sector ETFs are close – those that track energy, communications services, and consumer discretionary stocks. Meanwhile, the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLRE) is the best example of an “undecided” one, trading right around breakeven.

What message can we take away from this trading action? I’d characterize it as fairly bullish. Defensive groups like staples and healthcare are underperforming. Offensive groups like tech and discretionary are outperforming. Energy is also mounting a late-quarter rally – which could signal some relief for investors in that lagging corner of the commodities market.

Or in other words... It’s still a "Be Bold" market.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CRE Debt Outstanding Climbs In Lending's Golden Era
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy

The details

The Mortgage Bankers Association released its 2Q25 Commercial/Multifamily Mortgage Debt Outstanding Report on September 23, 2025.

The level of commercial/multifamily mortgage debt outstanding increased by $47.1 billion (1%) in the second quarter of 2025, bringing the total debt outstanding to about $4.88 trillion. Within that, multifamily mortgage debt alone rose by $27.7 billion (1.3%) to $2.19 trillion from the first quarter of 2025. This implies that core commercial mortgage debt increased by $19.4 billion (0.7%) to approximately $2.69 trillion compared to the prior quarter. This is consistent with the Mortgage Bankers Association’s 2Q25 Survey of Commercial/Multifamily Mortgage Banker Originations, which previously showed that originations rose 48% quarter-over-quarter and 66% year-over-year.

Among the largest lenders, life insurance companies and REITs experienced the greatest quarter-over-quarter increases in total commercial/multifamily mortgage debt outstanding—up 2.4% and 2.2%, respectively. REITs had the largest increase in the prior quarter, underscoring the growing importance of debt funds in commercial real estate lending markets.

Only three lender types saw quarter-over-quarter declines:

  • Nonfinancial Corporate Business (-0.2%)
  • Finance companies (-1.2%)
  • State and Local Government Retirement Funds (-1.9%)

However, it’s important to note that these lender categories represent relatively small portions of the total mortgage debt outstanding—just 2.4%, 0.7%, and 0.1%, respectively.

Notably, banks and thrifts continue to increase their total outstanding mortgage debt holdings by 0.9%, now accounting for 37.5% of the total market. This trend is likely driven by large banks, as the Federal Reserve’s Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey indicated that large banks reported stronger loan demand in the second quarter, while smaller banks experienced weaker demand.

Commercial and Multifamily Mortgage Debt Outstanding

Quarterly, by sector

Bottom line

The increase in outstanding CRE mortgage debt across both multifamily and core commercial underscores that debt capital markets remain liquid, supported by a broad mix of lenders. Looking ahead, we view CRE lending as not only resilient but also one of the most compelling opportunities across the four quadrants of real estate investing, with potential for attractive absolute, volatility-adjusted, and risk-adjusted returns. With higher rates and elevated maturities creating a favorable environment for lenders and pricing still compelling relative to other risk assets, we believe now is the time to lean into CRE debt as a differentiated source of return.

Original Post

data.title: Altisource Portfolio Solutions: From Broken Trust To Strong Buy
data.description:
Altisource Portfolio Solutions S.A. (NASDAQ:ASPS) is a “Strong Buy”. Over a decade since the share price collapsed thanks to revelations about the company’s misdeeds, current management has been able to steer the company toward growth, improved profitability, and better cost discipline. In addition, macro tailwinds are very favourable for profitability. With the company still trading at a fraction of what the sector is, there is enormous upside to the share price.

Background

Altisource serves the real estate and mortgage industries as an integrated service provider and marketplace, offering a comprehensive suite of solutions through its two primary reportable segments: the Servicer and Estate segment and the Origination segment.

The Servicer and Real Estate segment provides solutions such as title insurance, real estate valuation services, and residential real estate renovation services; serves as a marketplace for auctioning, real estate brokerage and as well as asset management; and offers technology and Software-as-a-Service ((SaaS)) products for things like managing real estate owned and investor homes, handling invoicing and payments, and commercial loan servicing.

The Origination segment provides solutions for the entirety of the mortgage origination cycle, such as solutions for title insurance and services settlement, and loan fulfillment; supports mortgage bankers and originators with platforms like Lenders One Loan Automation; and offers technology and SaaS products for things like vendor management, ordering services and automating parts of the loan manufacturing process, and managing documents and delivering data analytics.

The company’s diversified service portfolio positions the company as a critical behind-the-scenes operator in the mortgage and real estate ecosystem, serving financial institutions, government-sponsored enterprises, asset managers, and real estate investors. Indeed, the company’s service revenue is its most important revenue category, with the firm also earning revenue from reimbursable expenses and non-controlling interests. Altisource’s business model makes it a beneficiary in the current refinance origination surge and potential future increases in foreclosure activity.

Stock Market Failure and Broken Trust

Usually, when analysing a business, the recent past is all that seems to matter. Yet, for Altisource, it is arguable that the firm still suffers from an event that occurred over a decade ago. Since its August 2009 IPO, Altisource’s share price has declined by 89.44%, and the stock has generated -89.01% in total returns, compared to a share price gain of 545.23% and total returns of 766.87% for the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) and a share price gain of 529.60% and total returns of 722.25% for the iShares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV). As the chart shows, the stock has never recovered from its 30 November 2013 crash, a crash seemingly triggered by revelations that the New York Department of Financial Services (NYDFS) was investigating the company regarding misrepresentations about its relationship with Ocwen Financial.

Once Altisource’s credibility took a massive hit, and its growth assumptions were challenged, the company could not easily rebuild the narrative that had driven its lofty valuation. Prior to the crash, the company’s share price had shot up 1,089%. Over the years, with continued regulatory pressure, legal exposure, debt stress, and a weaker business model, the stock has not only never fully regained its prior highs, it has continued a slow decline.

Altisource would later reach a $32 million settlement with its shareholders, and the NYDFS would levy a $150 million fine. A quote from the final complaint by investors highlights the extent of misdeeds:

“To assure investors and regulators that the two companies did not engage in self-dealing transactions that would harm Ocwen’s borrowers, both Ocwen and the Altisource Defendants publicly represented to Altisource investors that Defendants managed the conflicts of interest posed by Erbey’s [Ocwen’s founder] leadership role at and financial interest in the related companies. Specifically, Altisource, Ocwen and Erbey represented that active steps were taken to manage Erbey’s conflicts, including through “oversight” by the “independent” members of each company’s Board of Directors – a representation that assured investors that Erbey was not engaged in devising, negotiating and approving the terms of transactions between the related companies.

Moreover, Defendants and Ocwen explicitly stated that Erbey recused himself from transactions involving the two companies to ensure the absence of conflicts and self-dealing. All of these representations embodied an effective commitment to investors that Defendants were protecting Altisource investors by prohibiting Erbey’s involvement in transactions between companies where he had a financial stake in both companies.

In reality – and in stark contrast to Defendants’ Class Period statements to Altisource investors – Altisource and Ocwen, at Erbey’s direction, engaged in conflicted related party transactions designed to improperly funnel money from innocent homeowners to Altisource and Erbey. Every aspect of this fraud has now been admitted by Ocwen. When the truth of Defendants’ Class Period statements was finally revealed, Altisource’s common stock had lost a total of over $1 billion in market capitalization.”

Altisource has spent years rebuilding its reputation and untangling itself from the fallout of this period, with revenue and profitability declining.

The Cycle Has Turned

Altisource’s revenue declined from $365.55 million in 2020 to $168.27 million in the trailing twelve months (TTM). This brings to mind an excellent analysis on Seeking Alpha in which an analyst in 2020 forecast declining revenues, urging investors to wait before investing in the company, because the cycle had not yet turned, and the company was still dealing with the fallout of the Ocwen business. Since that analyst report, the stock has declined 92.64%. In the TTM, however, the stock has gone up 28.83%, and in the year-to-date (YTD), the stock is up 117.52%. That rise in the stock price is due to what seems to be a turning in the company’s cycle, with revenue up from $160.13 million in 2024.

The company’s profitability has improved as well, with gross profit margins rising from 16.5% in 2020 to 30.23% in the TTM, and net losses narrowing from -$67.16 million in 2020 to -$6.89 million in the TTM, with TTM net losses narrowing from -$35.64 million in 2024. Chairman and Chief Executive Officer William B. Shepro, remarked in the Q2 2025 earnings release that,

“We are pleased with our second quarter performance. In a close to historically low delinquency environment, we grew Service revenue, Adjusted EBITDA(1), pre- and post-tax GAAP earnings and GAAP earnings per share compared to the second quarter of last year. This is largely from our focus on growing our businesses that have tailwinds, cost discipline, lower interest expense and the reversal of certain tax reserves related to our India operations.

To support longer term growth, we are focusing our efforts on accelerating the growth of those businesses that we believe have tailwinds in what remains a close to historically low delinquency environment. Should loan delinquencies, foreclosure starts and foreclosure sales increase, we believe we are well positioned to also benefit from stronger revenue and Adjusted EBITDA(1) growth in our largest and most profitable countercyclical businesses.”

Driving this change has been stronger service volumes due to a surge in refinance origination industry-wide and new business wins, improved margins in the two operating segments, Servicer and Real Estate & Origination, and corporate cost/efficiency actions that have reduced overhead losses. The company’s Q2 2025 service revenue looks to be the biggest the company has earned since Q3 2021. In fact, growth has been accelerating for several quarters. In Q4 2024, management celebrated service revenue of $38.4 million, which was also higher than Q3 2021 service revenue.

In February of this year, management closed an exchange and maturity extension transaction with its lenders, a move that Shepro described as “significantly strengthening our balance sheet and reducing interest expense. We ended the quarter with $30.8 million in unrestricted cash.” Since then, the firm’s unrestricted cash has declined to $30 million. If interest rates continue to decline, which seems probable, profitability will improve.

It has helped that delinquencies are at historically low levels. Data from the Federal Reserve Database (FRED) shows that 90-plus day mortgage delinquency rates are indeed at what Shepro referred to as “near historic lows”, at 1.79% as of Q2 2025, although there is an uptick. Foreclosure starts in the first half of the year rose 5.8% from the same period in 2024.

Mortgage origination volume has grown since Q1 2023, with management citing this growth as evidence for favorable industry-wide demand for the services Altisource provides, namely originations, valuation, title, closing/settlement, and supporting services. This has been key to driving the growth in service revenue.

Valuation is Very Attractive

If these factors continue, we should expect Altisource’s price/sales multiple to converge toward that of its peers and the sector medians. Currently, the company has a price/sales multiple of 0.43, compared to Seeking Alpha's sector median of 4.70, and Aswath Damodaran’s sector median of 1.28. The company’s peers, Offerpad Solutions Inc. (OPAD), Fathom Holdings Inc. (FTHM), Ohmyhome Limited (OMH), RE/MAX Holdings, Inc. (RMAX), and The Real Brokerage Inc. (REAX), have an average price/sales ratio of 0.38.

Price/Sales Multiple Source

Target Price = Revenue per Share Estimate × Price/Sales

Upside/(Downside) from $11.44

Damodaran

$ 33.74

194.94%

Sector Median

$ 123.89

982.97%

Peers

$ 10.02

-12.44%

Equally Weighted Target

$ 78.82

588.95%

Source: Seeking Alpha, Damodaran, and author calculations

As the table above shows, this gives an enormous upside of 588.95% from the current price, the price/sales improves toward an equally-weighted blended average of peer and sector multiples.

The macro condition is perhaps the most important reason to believe that at least some of this potential upside will be realised. In the Q2 2025 earnings call and earnings presentation, management noted that despite 90+ day mortgage delinquency rates being at historically low levels, foreclosure starts have increased. This is likely, management believes, because of the expiration of the Department of Veterans Affairs (VA) foreclosure moratorium. The VA moratorium was a pandemic-era protection for veterans with VA-backed loans, and its expiration has begun to release a pipeline of foreclosure work that directly benefits service providers like Altisource. This analysis seems to be backed up by independent data, with Intercontinental Exchange, Inc. (ICE) observing a surge in foreclosure starts and sales in the aftermath of the expiration.

Management is optimistic about the impact of improving business conditions on its revenue, saying:

“Should loan delinquencies, foreclosure starts and foreclosure sales increase, we believe we are well positioned to benefit from stronger Service revenue and Adjusted EBITDA growth in our largest and most profitable countercyclical businesses.”

The origination market has also continued to show strength, with Q2 2025 mortgage origination rising 27% year-over-year, with purchase volume increasing 5% and refinancing activating increasing by 89%. The Mortgage Bankers Association's (MBA) projection of 5.6 million loans to be originated in 2025 provides another tangible, industry-vetted baseline for continued demand and therefore, an increase in Altisource’s revenue.

Importantly, my valuation model assumes that revenue per share remains constant. If revenue increases, then the market is likely to re-rate the firm upwards. So, while management provides no guidance, management’s discussion of their results and the outlook suggests that they are confident revenue will increase, and if that happens, the market will respond positively.

Altisource Remains Fragile

With $30 million in cash and $196.54 million in total debt, Altisource has a deeply negative net cash position of -$166.56 million. The company’s 0.33 interest coverage ratio indicates that operating income is enough to cover just a third of interest obligations, which leaves it vulnerable to rising interest rates. At present, that seems a distant threat, given that the Fed is more likely to leave rates as they are or cut them.

It is important to acknowledge that macro conditions are an important piece of this thesis, and so, if delinquencies, interest rate cuts, and foreclosure activity do not go the way Altisource needs, recent progress may be undermined.

Conclusion

Altisource has endured a decade to forget, in which the company has struggled with the fallout of the Ocwen affair, revenues have fallen, profitability has declined and the reputation of the firm has taken a hit. That said, there seems to have been a turn in the last year, as growth, improving profitability, macro tailwinds, and cost discipline have made the business that much stronger. Markets have responded strongly to these changes, and there still remains a lot of upside given how cheaply the company trades compared to the sector. This makes Altisource a “Strong Buy”.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - Getting Ready For September NFP Week
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Week in review – UN Assembly, ever-stronger metals, Powell and a huge USD performance,

A week dominated by central bank communications and seasonal dynamics kept investors on edge, even as the UN General Assembly passed without major geopolitical surprises except for some memorable US President Trump quotes.

Instead, markets focused on Fed officials and their global counterparts weighing in on shifting economic conditions.

Powell’s Rhode Island appearance delivered another shift to Market focus, highlighting rising downside risks to employment and how hiring momentum has “dropped very sharply.”

Fed Governor Bowman, a known dove, pushed the point further, calling for a more proactive stance to avoid falling "behind the curve."

Further north, Bank of Canada Governor Macklem stressed that trade is under attack, citing a sharp slowdown in exports and weaker investment trends tied to US policy uncertainty.

He also had a few words to say on the Fed’s independence and shed some light on Jerome Powell’s solid performance amid all the menaces.

Markets responded in seasonal fashion, following the classic “sell Rosh Hashanah” adage pattern as volumes thinned and equities corrected from recent highs, leading to a three-day correction in Equities.

Happy New Year to those who celebrate, and peace to markets and communities alike.

Powell’s reminder that “equity prices are fairly highly valued” added pressure, with the S&P 500 and broader indices retracing into familiar September headwinds.

The US Dollar, however, stood out, rallying on strong technical catalysts and better-than-expected US data that pushed back against optimistic 2026 rate cut bets.

Resilient job numbers, as seen in the latest Jobless Claims report, and decent macro conditions for now helped the greenback regain ground, underscoring the relative strength of the US economy against global peers.

A tense vibe can also be seen in the latest headline from US Secretary of War Pete Hegseth, who is convening all army generals next week.

This vibe actually might have been one of the reasons for such a huge performance in metals, with Gold again marking fresh record highs on Tuesday, Platinum reaching 12-year highs and Silver pushing to reach new all-time highs, breaching $46 just today.

Weekly performance from different asset classes

As mentioned in the introduction, metals and the US Dollar have both sustained a consistent, strong performance, but the commodity that stood out the most was easily Oil.

Black gold silently rose close to 5% just this week and closing the week at its highs. It seems that Europe turning away from Russian oil as this theme progresses is starting to have an influence on the pricing itself.

The real outperformers of the week, to the downside unfortunately, have been cryptocurrencies, which took a dent to their strong yearly performance.

They maintain at fairly high levels, but their price action resembles more one of a small correction rather than a simple pullback: Ethereum now stands below the $4,000 mark, and Bitcoin is closing the week shy of $110,000.

Let's also see if a further correction in cryptocurrencies could trigger further reactions to the current stubbornly ecstatic market sentiment.

Read More:

  • Dow Jones rises, but major support is under threat
  • Dollar strength rattles global Markets: what to watch for the USD

Let’s dive into next week’s action, with a week that should be pretty busy.

The Week Ahead – NFP week and RBA meeting

Asia Pacific Markets - A new RBNZ Governor and a well-anticipated RBA meeting

Not mentioned in the introduction, but Markets are welcoming the new Royal Bank of New Zealand Governor Anna Breman. The week in Asia-Pacific kicks off with Japanese retail trade data and China’s official PMIs on Monday, but the week really starts the following day for APAC trading.

The main focus lands squarely on Tuesday, when the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) delivers its interest rate decision, statement, and rate outlook, followed by Governor Bullock’s press conference.

The market is largely priced for a pause, with only 6% of a cut priced in and only 16 bps of cut premiums priced through the end of 2025.

Later in the day, Japan’s Tankan survey offers fresh insights into business sentiment, while New Zealand reports on building permits.

On Wednesday, Australia publishes its monthly trade balance, exports, and imports, alongside the RBA’s Financial Stability Review, keeping AUD traders busy for the week. As a matter of fact, the week doesn't end here for Australian data.

Thursday turns to high-frequency indicators with Australia’s S&P Global PMIs and Japan’s unemployment rate, while Bank of Japan Governor Ueda’s speech will be closely watched for hints on upcoming policy.

Indeed, more talks around a hawkish BoJ are starting to appear, with data corroborating a potential future hike at the October 30th rate decision.

Remember that Bank of Japan rate decisions are huge for markets, particularly regarding Basis trades.

The week rounds off on Friday with Japan’s ruling LDP presidential election — a political event that could indirectly shape the BoJ’s stance and the yen outlook under new party leadership.

US, Europe and UK Markets - US Non-Farm Payrolls, UK GDP, Swiss CPI and many speakers

The week starts with an army of central bank voices on Monday, as both the Fed (Williams, Waller...) and the ECB unleash a wave of speakers, setting the tone for rates and policy expectations.

Since the September meetings have passed, markets now await what CB speakers will have to say regarding upcoming decisions and what they are looking at.

On Tuesday, attention shifts to the UK with Q2 GDP (QoQ/YoY) and to the eurozone with German CPI and euro-wide HICP prints, crucial for inflation watchers. ECB President Lagarde also speaks at the Bank of Finland’s 4th International Monetary Policy Conference

Wednesday brings more eurozone inflation data, this time the core HICP, alongside the US ADP employment change and ISM Manufacturing PMI — a solid preview into Friday’s labour market release.

On Thursday, Switzerland releases its CPI report, potentially steering CHF volatility, particularly with the ongoing deflation and the SNB hinting that they would be ready to cut rates to negative territory if needed.

The week’s climax lands on Friday with the US Non-Farm Payrolls, wages data, and unemployment rate for the month of September — this report will be essential as per usual, as the mood seems to relax regarding US employment: the recent decreases could be related to a lower labor demand from tighter immigration rather than an actual economic slowdown.

But always remember that NFP can bring some surprises.

Of course, don't forget the ISM Services PMI at 10:00 A.M. that day.

Not in the picture, but do not forget the Chinese NBS Manufacturing PMI data on Monday evening (21:30).

Safe trades, and enjoy your weekend!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Down 5% In September Amid Economic Concerns
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment fell for a second straight month, hitting its lowest level since May, amid increasing economic concerns. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index was down 3.1 points, or about 5%, to 55.1 in September. The latest reading was lower than the forecast of 55.4. Consumer sentiment has fallen by 21.4% (15.0 points) compared to a year ago.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regard to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month: a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment confirmed its early-month reading and eased about 5% from last month but remains above the low readings seen in April and May of this year. Although September’s decline was relatively modest, it was still seen across a broad swath of the population, across groups by age, income, and education, and all five index components. A key exception: sentiment for consumers with larger stock holdings held steady in September, while for those with smaller or no holdings, sentiment decreased. This month, sentiment moved down about 9% for independents and 4% for Republicans, whereas it lifted this month for Democrats. Nationally, not only did macroeconomic expectations fall, particularly for labor markets and business conditions, but personal expectations did as well, with a softening outlook for their own incomes and personal finances. Consumers continue to express frustration over the persistence of high prices, with 44% spontaneously mentioning that high prices are eroding their personal finances, the highest reading in a year. Interviews this month highlight the fact that consumers feel pressure both from the prospect of higher inflation as well as the risk of weaker labor markets.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. At 55.1, the current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 34.6% below its average reading of 84.3 (arithmetic mean) and 33.7% below its geometric mean of 83.1, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 1st percentile of the 573 monthly data points in this series.

To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In September, the CECI fell for a second straight month to 60.4, its lowest level since May. This represents a 2.1% decline from the previous month and a 4.6% drop from a year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 61.2.

Meanwhile, the CEI fell for a third straight month to 51.7, its lowest level since May. This represents a 7.5% decline from the previous month and a 30.5% drop from one year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 51.8.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations receded slightly to 4.7% from 4.8% last month. Long-run inflation expectations moved up for the second straight month to 3.7% in September, but stand much lower than the 4.4% spike seen in April.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Revolve Group: Interesting Growth Story, Not Undervalued Now
data.description:
The small-cap world is full of unique companies, and Revolve is surely one. Revolve has proven itself a success domestically, but the share price assumes international expansion is a sure thing. That growth hasn’t materialized yet. I think the company will struggle to deliver against those assumptions, making the current price too high for the risk.

Revolve Group Inc (NYSE:RVLV) is an American online clothing retailer. They were founded in 2003 and went public in 2019. Below is their share price performance since IPO:

Their two operating segments are REVOLVE and FRWD. The first offers trend-focused clothing, shoes, accessories, and beauty products aimed at Millennials and Gen Z, mixing its own labels with brands like Free People and Levi’s. FWRD acts as the luxury side of the business, featuring a curated selection from high-end designers such as Saint Laurent, Isabel Marant, and Balenciaga.

Below is a comparison of their return metrics with peers. This is another case where there are basically no 100% ‘like-for-like’ competitors in the public markets:

They are a 100% online company, with zero retail presence. They are known for free shipping and free returns. The returns don’t extend unlimited generosity, though. It’s not the same as Wal-Mart offering hassle-free returns in order to garner customer goodwill over time, ensuring the customers will return. Returns are a big deal, and while roughly 50% of online clothing purchases are returned, RVLV isn’t quite at that level, but it’s not far behind.

Capital Allocation

Below is a look at how capital has been allocated for the past decade:

Year

2017

Source

They have a capital-light business model. They don’t own their factories and don’t operate any retail locations. Most major functions are outsourced. Even the production of their own in-house designs is done by 3rd parties. This gives them the flexibility to test certain designs in smaller batches before ramping up production.

So in the strictest sense, OpeX is more critical than Capex due to the marketing strategy they pursue. They use paid influencers to advertise online and at events. The ROI for putting on a promotional event is a bit opaque, because these events are purposefully done for awareness, not simply how much merch can be sold in a single night. In other words, the payoff comes farther down the line.

My point here is that RVLV is still in a phase where capital allocation isn’t the main focus. Getting the OpEx part right in regard to not overspending or underspending on live marketing is still vital for the company’s success right now. So overall, their capital allocation is fine, and as you can see in the table, they put emphasis on repurchases the past 2 years, which is a trend I would like to see continue.

I also like that SBC is kept to a minimum. Most tech/online companies use a lot of SBC, but RVLV deserves some praise for keeping it low.

Risk

I’d like to start this section by saying that I always strive to be a bottoms-up stock picker, without letting macro events drive my decision making. In the case of RVLV however, one can’t ignore the macro realities.

They sell discretionary clothing to a very particular segment. RVLV’s ideal customer in the US is affluent, urban females from roughly age 18-35. With zero retail presence, the only people learning of RVLV’s products are the ones who are knee-deep in social media. Most of their marketing is done via paying influencers to wear their clothing on social media and to attend promotional events.

They collect vast amounts of data, which is valuable to them, not because they sell it; they use it to improve their own selection. Part of the value-add of RVLV is a curated selection instead of endless scrolling through vast options on Amazon or FB marketplace.

The way they expand is not to change their ideal customer in the US but to replicate the US experience in other countries. They have been slowly dipping their toes into the international markets. These markets are being tested by the company; they are very methodical about the speed at which they expand.

Tariff’s do pose an issue for the company, but nothing that will sink them or cause severe damage. Having premium priced products actually helps in the sense that rising prices are generally expected anyways. RVLV's ideal customer is more affluent and would feel the effects of price increases far less than, say, an average WMT shopper dealing with pricing increases. This doesn't mean, however, that they have tons of pricing power as a long-term driver of returns.

Should we enter even tougher economic circumstances, RVLV will be forced to pivot in certain ways, but their business model provides sufficient cushion. With no physical stores, they aren't locked into multi-year leases, and they don't have to make the tough decisions of how many stores to open or close. They can adjust prices to attract different customers if they really need to.

Balance sheet risk is extremely low; long-term debt sits at $28 million, while cash and cash equivalents are at $311 million. They mildly diluted for the first few years as a public company, but SBC was always kept low, and the past two years saw a slight reduction in share count.

Earnings

Let's take a quick look at recent earnings:

In the most recent quarter, I was a bit surprised to see YoY revenue up as much as it was. I also don’t expect strong results for next quarter, because the average US consumer is not very strong right now. I'm more interested in the longer-run results of their international efforts, and this takes time to play out.

Valuation

Shares reached an all-time high back in the everything bubble of 2021. They then suffered a 85% decline over the next two years. From that point, shares rose 218% a little over a year later and have since declined 44% to the current price.

Let's start with a look at historical and then current multiples:

The historical charts, however, do indicate the level of hyper-optimism back in 2021. It appears priced for slower growth right now. This makes complete sense due to the slowing revenue growth over the past three years.

Company

EV/Sales

EV/EBITDA

EV/FCF

P/B

RVLV

1.2

22

22

3.7

ZLNDY

0.5

7.3

18.2

29.1

ASOMF

0.2

-5.2

5.8

1.2

SFIX

0.4

-12.2

45.8

3.7

As mentioned earlier, these public peers aren't exact replicas, and for that reason, comparing the multiples against competitors doesn't help us on a relative basis, but it's still safe to look at RVLV's multiples as pricing and as general market sentiment.

It's important to consider the stage of the life cycle when valuing RVLV. They are far beyond hypergrowth and into slower growth as they reach the peak of their realistic TAM in America. Future growth will come from international expansion. I project EPS growth assuming modest, not stellar, success internationally. Let's take a look at the DCF model now:

I based my growth assumptions on a balance of optimism about Revolve’s differentiated, capital-light model and skepticism around the pace of international expansion. While the company has carved out a unique niche and demonstrated strong profitability, I modeled earnings to grow at a modest 10% annually over the next decade, reflecting steady U.S. performance and limited overseas traction, before dropping to a conservative 1% terminal rate. It should also be kept in mind that the record breaking operating profit and EPS in 2021 were purely because of the circumstances of the pandemic; they shouldn't become the new hurdle rate.

I appreciate the fact that they are slow and cautious with international expansion, but I think the market is pricing in too much optimism, as if the international game is a sure thing for them. It certainly isn’t. Execution risk is still relatively high. They’ve proven out the model in the US completely, but replicating it elsewhere might require different leadership with a different skill set.

At the end of the day, getting the international game right isn’t make or break, but it does determine if RVLV will continue being a growth company or become a return of capital company.

Despite the current price, I think it's qualitatively worth watching. They’ve built a solid brand overall in the US, and are much better positioned to pivot in worse economic times compared to brick-and-mortar retailers. The international question hasn’t been answered conclusively yet, and the market cap doesn’t account for this.

Conclusion

RVLV is a unique online clothing retailer, with no exact clone in the public markets. It's still in the growth phase, and executing marketing is more important than capital allocation decisions at this point in time. They've done a good job of creating a brand and serving a particular niche. I have my doubts about how successful international expansion will be, as they've only dipped their toes into the water.

I give the stock a "hold" rating right now, but it's worth watching for those with a qualitative and growth bent. It's a very unique and impressive example of a capital-light + highly outsourced business model.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 09/27/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: 6 Upcoming Dividend Increases Including A King
data.description:
Welcome back to another edition of upcoming dividend increases. I'll focus on dividend increases for the week of September 28th, 2025. I encourage investors to consider the power of dividend stocks, your pathway to steady and increasing income. There's nothing like sitting back and collecting regular paychecks from everyday companies simply by holding their shares. Adopting a dividend growth strategy encourages your returns to grow, making your financial future brighter. Now is a great time to invest in dividend stocks and enjoy the financial rewards they bring.

Companies that regularly raise their dividends tend to be great long-term investments. Dividend increases suggest financial strength and stability, indicating that a company can generate reliable cash flow. It also requires deliberate capital allocation planning by management to make it a core tenet of the business. Consistent dividend growth typically indicates that a company is generating reliable cash flow, which in turn enables it to offer investors a combination of income and capital appreciation.

From the broader universe of investable stocks, I focus on those with a proven track record of annual dividend increases. I watch dividend increase announcements and combine those with other supporting data points. This information is core to my investment process, and I share it here with the Seeking Alpha community to help you make more informed investment decisions.

This week, I'll spotlight another group of companies and provide more detail on both the selections and the methodology behind them.

A few highlights from this week's list:

  • Average dividend increase: 7.8% (median: 7.6%)

  • Average dividend streak: 26 years (median: 19 years)

  • Dividend King Illinois Tool Works extends its 62-year streak with a 7.3% increase.

My strategy focuses on buying, holding, and adding to positions in companies that raise their dividends annually and have the potential to outperform relevant benchmarks over time.

How I Created The Lists

The information presented here is the result of combining multiple data sources: the "U.S. Dividend Champions" spreadsheet provides the universe of companies I review, and then upcoming dividend announcements. This combination joins data on companies with a track record of consistent dividend growth and the timeliness of their dividend increases. It's important to note that every company on this list has a minimum of 5 years of dividend growth history.

To be considered for this list, a company must offer higher total annual dividends. Although a company might not increase its dividend every calendar year, its overall annual dividend can still grow.

What Is The Ex-Dividend Date?

The ex-dividend date is when you must own shares to qualify for an upcoming dividend or distribution. To be eligible, you must have bought the shares by the end of the preceding business day. For instance, if the ex-dividend date is Tuesday, you must have acquired the shares by the market close on Monday. If the ex-dividend date falls on a Monday (or a Tuesday following a holiday on Monday), you must have purchased the shares by the previous Friday.

Dividend Streak Categories

Here are the definitions of the streak categories that I'll use throughout the piece.

  • King: 50+ years.
  • Champion/Aristocrat: 25+ years.
  • Contender: 10-24 years.
  • Challenger: 5+ years.
Category Count
King 1
Champion 1
Contender 4
Challenger 0

The Dividend Increases List

The data is sorted by the ex-dividend date (ascending) and then by the streak (descending):

Name Ticker Streak Forward Yield Ex-Div Date Increase Percent Streak Category
Illinois Tool Works Inc. (ITW) 62 2.48 30-Sep-25 7.33% King
BancFirst Corporation (BANF) 32 1.5 30-Sep-25 6.52% Champion
Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB) 15 3.54 30-Sep-25 8.11% Contender
Mondelez International, Inc. - Class A Common Stock (MDLZ) 12 3.1 30-Sep-25 6.38% Contender
State Street Corporation (STT) 15 2.97 1-Oct-25 10.53% Contender
Republic Services, Inc. (RSG) 22 1.1 2-Oct-25 7.76% Contender

Field Definitions

Streak: Years of dividend growth history are sourced from the U.S. Dividend Champions spreadsheet.

Forward Yield: The payout rate is calculated by dividing the new payout rate by the current share price.

Ex-Dividend Date: This is the date by which you must own the stock to receive the dividend.

Increase Percent: The percent increase.

Streak Category: This is the company's overall dividend history classification.

Show Me The Money

Here is a table that shows the new and old rates and the percentage increase. The table is sorted by ex-dividend day in ascending order and dividend streak in descending order.

Ticker Old Rate New Rate Increase Percent
ITW 1.5 1.61 7.33%
BANF 0.46 0.49 6.52%
FITB 0.37 0.4 8.11%
MDLZ 0.47 0.5 6.38%
STT 0.76 0.84 10.53%
RSG 0.58 0.63 7.76%

Additional Metrics

Here are additional metrics related to these companies. Some data points include yearly pricing action and the P/E ratio. The table is sorted the same way as the table above.

Ticker Current Price 52 Week Low 52 Week High PE Ratio % Off Low % Off High
ITW 260.1 214.66 279.13 30.76 21% Off Low 7% Off High
BANF 130.84 97.02 138.77 18.69 35% Off Low 6% Off High
FITB 45.14 32.25 49.07 13.67 40% Off Low 8% Off High
MDLZ 64.43 53.95 75.11 26.86 19% Off Low 14% Off High
STT 113.1 72.81 116.37 12.12 55% Off Low 3% Off High
RSG 228.08 197.61 258.75 29.73 15% Off Low 12% Off High

Tickers By Yield And Growth Rates

I've organized the table in descending order, allowing investors to prioritize the current yield. Additionally, the table includes some historical dividend growth rates. I've also incorporated the "Chowder Rule," the sum of the current yield and the five-year dividend growth rate.

Ticker Yield 1 Yr DG 3 Yr DG 5 Yr DG 10 Yr DG Chowder Rule
FITB 3.54 5.7 7.3 7.7 11 10.9
MDLZ 3.1 10.6 10.3 10.5 12.1 13.7
STT 2.97 43.5 9.2 7.4 9.1 10.4
ITW 2.48 7.2 7.1 7 12 9.3
BANF 1.5 7 8.5 7.5 10.5 8.9
RSG 1.1 41.8 7.3 7 7.4 8

Historical Returns

My investment strategy focuses on identifying stocks that consistently outperform the market and grow their dividend payouts over time. For broad exposure to U.S. equity markets, excluding the REIT sector, I recommend the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

SCHD features strong historical performance, offers a yield that exceeds that of the S&P 500, and consistently delivers increasing dividends. With over $70 in assets, it's an incredibly popular dividend-growth ETF. The ten-year dividend growth rate is one of the four key factors that SCHD tracks in its index.

Based on this analysis and further due diligence, I have included several companies in my portfolio and will sometimes make timely purchases of existing holdings. The charts below assume dividends are reinvested.

Our benchmark SCHD returned 215% over the past decade. As I mentioned above, I lose interest in individual holdings if they can't at least match that. Right off the bat, STT and MDLZ are not attractive to me with their 120% and 82% returns, respectively.

Now, the other four individual companies have all outperformed SCHD, which is what I'll now focus on.

FITB returned 232%, ITW 295%, BANF 415%, and our big winner this week is RSG with a total return of 551% over the past ten years.

Next Steps

I'm very selective about the individual holdings in my portfolio. The individual sleeve portion of my portfolio has 33 equal-weight holdings. Since that sleeve already accounts for a percentage of my overall portfolio, there are definitely diminishing returns to holding any additional assets.

I'm looking for companies that offer significant alpha over SCHD and stand out from it. Furthermore, I believe in the importance of consistent, strong earnings and dividend growth over time. These articles provide me (and I hope you) a chance to review what's performed well and to take a closer look at my current holdings.

I would say I'm interested in RSG, but I already own Waste Management (WM). Those two stocks trade very similarly, so there's no additional benefit of adding another one. But there are definitely some interesting performers on the list this week.

I'd love to hear your thoughts on my strategy, so feel free to share yours in the comments below! As always, do your due diligence before making any investment decisions.

data.title: Global Medical REIT: Why I Skipped The Commons And 7.4% Yielding Preferreds
data.description:
Global Medical REIT (NYSE:GMRE) faces heavy near-term refinancing liabilities that will place pressure on its funds from operations ("FFO") and could work to dampen its dividend coverage. This renders a position in the common shares as a tougher sell, even with an elevated dividend yield. GMRE focuses on healthcare facilities leased to physician groups and regional and national healthcare systems. The REIT engineered a one-for-five reverse stock split last month, which is important to note for the adjustment of the per-share metrics. Essentially, multiply the pre-reverse stock split metric by 5. GMRE last reported a quarterly cash dividend of $0.75 per share, around $0.15 per share pre-reverse stock split. The figure annualizes at $3 per share for an 8.9% dividend yield. GMRE had previously cut the payout by 29% in May, breaking away from what had been stable distributions since 2016. The REIT also has publicly traded preferred shares; the 7.50% Series A Cumulative Redeemable Preferreds (NYSE:GMRE.PR.A), which pays out a $1.88 annual coupon for a 7.4% dividend yield. Which is the better buy? Likely the common shares, but I'm not a fan of either security.

Firstly, the preferreds are trading above their $25 per share liquidation value by around 1.88%. While this isn't a material divergence, my investment style tilts towards preferreds trading at deep discounts. This helps lock in a margin of safety and builds upside exposure in the event the negative divergence to liquidation value narrows. For example, my position in Highland Income Fund's Series A 5.375% Preferreds (HFRO.PR.A) was built when the preferreds were trading at a 38.7% discount to liquidation value. This discount has since slimmed to 32.7%, helping my portfolio lock in capital gains beyond the current dividend yield. GMRE's preferreds are also perpetual, and are trading past their September 2022 call date. These were issued back in 2017 when the Fed funds rate was much lower.

Why Was The Dividend Cut?

GMRE reported fiscal 2025 second-quarter revenue of $37.97 million, up 10.9% over its year-ago comp and a beat by $2.4 million on consensus estimates. This drove FFO of $1 per share for the second quarter, in line with consensus and flat from FFO of $1 per share a year ago. GMRE also realized first-quarter 2025 FFO of $1 per share. The REIT's dividend coverage currently stands at 133%, or a roughly 75% payout ratio. This is well below the 90% requirement for a REIT to pay out, providing GMRE with more headroom following the cut. Further, the REIT is guiding for full-year fiscal 2025 FFO of $4.45 to $4.65 per share, meaning 152% coverage of its annualized dividend of $3 per share at the midpoint.

The cut made sense because GMRE was previously paying out a dividend that was more than it earned in FFO. The current relative depth of coverage does dampen the need for further cuts, but the REIT's constrained cash position does provide a reason for pause. GMRE's cash and cash equivalents ended the second quarter at $9.23 million, up from $7.8 million a year ago but still extremely underweighted against total debt of around $700 million. GMRE currently does not have to cut the dividend again, but with a weighted average interest rate of 4.09% on its debt and a term of 1.6 years, the REIT will be in a difficult position where it will have to refinance its debt at higher rates. This will likely be at a rate between the dividend yield on the common shares and the current weighted average interest rate on its debt. The REIT's weighted average rate on debt was up from 3.75% and a term of 2.0 years as of the start of its fiscal 2025.

Conclusion

GMRE's rental revenue was derived from 319 tenants with leases across 193 buildings, and no single tenant drove more than 10% of revenue. While this a positive for the REIT's risk profile, the extremely short-term maturity of its still cheap debt presents a significant barrier to taking a position in the commons. When aggregated with the recent dividend cut, the lack of progress on FFO growth, and the constrained cash position, GMRE does not currently present a compelling buy. Near-term Fed rate cuts will act to help lift sentiment on REIT's but even the most optimistic backdrop for rate cuts has 50 basis points more of cuts to end 2025 as the base scenario, keeping the Fed funds rate still relatively elevated ahead of GMRE's wall of refinancing. The ticker is a hold.

data.title: Circle: Poised To Thrive Even As Interest Rates Decline
data.description:
As the stock market falls from recent highs, one thought pervades my opportunistic mindset: which stocks, particularly momentum and growth stocks, have fallen sharper than their fundamentals might justify? What rebound opportunities exist in the crash?

While in general I regard most crypto and blockchain highfliers with skepticism, Circle Internet Group (NYSE:CRCL) caught my eye recently. The sponsor behind the number-two stablecoin, USDC, has now fallen well below its original $130 IPO price; despite one point reaching above $200 per share. As the stock recedes, the core question on investors’ minds is: can Circle pull off a rebound?

I’m initiating Circle at a buy rating. To address the elephant in the room: the core reason Circle has been falling recently is mostly the same reason other stocks are rising: lower interest rate expectations. Since this hurts Circle’s principal source of revenue (interest income), investors are rightly nervous. And yet I also think the sheer growth of USDC can’t be ignored. Use this dip as a buying opportunity.

The U.S. leader in regulated stablecoins

First, let’s start with what exactly Circle does. Circle is the sponsor behind one of the largest and best known stablecoins, USDC. For those new to the term, a stablecoin is a cryptocurrency that is pegged to the value of an underlying asset: in this case, 1:1 against the U.S. dollar. One of the best ways to think about stablecoins is fulfilling the original promise of crypto: by acting as an efficient means of exchange on the internet, minus the volatility and price fluctuation that has accompanied the speculative activity of other coins. Since there is no scarcity (like Bitcoin) and each USDC is backed by a real U.S. dollar held in reserve, the price does not move.

It’s probably not necessary to state that the stablecoin market is growing rapidly. As of the end of Q2, Circle noted that the market cap of stablecoins in circulation grew at a stunning 50% y/y clip to $223 billion. Transactions, meanwhile, grew at an even faster 75% y/y clip as more applications and merchants leverage blockchain technology to process payments. Stablecoins recently grew to more than 1% of U.S. M2 money supply.

The stablecoin industry as a whole saw a massive leap in adoption when the GENIUS Act was passed earlier this year by President Trump. GENIUS provided the first regulatory framework for oversight of stablecoins, including auditing reserve balances. Rather than strangle the industry with regulations, this was broadly seen as a positive for mainstream adoption.

USDC is only the second largest coin: USDT, or Tether, predates USDC and is roughly 2x larger. And yet regulation is the number one differentiator for USDC. Circle has taken a compliance first attitude toward USDC, while Tether, which is sponsored by a company domiciled in the British Virgin Islands, is less subject to regulation.

To me, and to many market observers, the regulatory safety net underpinning USDC is a major reason why Circle is poised to gain market share. The chart below showcases that USDC's share of crypto trading volumes has grown to 10%, up from just 1% in the year-ago quarter. This is just a beginning indication of the potential that USDC has to grow.

Key to USDC's market share expansion is a partnership with Binance, which is the largest crypto trading platform in the world. Since December 2024, Binance has made USDC more broadly available to its 240 million global customers. Since the partnership kicked off, USDC has also grown as a share of assets held in custody by Binance, as shown in the chart below:

Interest income: at risk as rates fall, but circulation growth should offset

Now let's turn our attention to the number-one factor that is pushing Circle stock down. Circle generates the lion's share of its revenue from interest held on the cash and short-term securities that back its issuance of USDC. As shown in the chart below, reserve returns have been falling alongside interest rate declines: in Q2, reserve returns of 4.14% (roughly matching current yields on U.S. short term treasury bonds, as measured by the SHV ETF (SHV)) fell -103 bps y/y.

After the close of the quarter, the Fed cut rates by a quarter point, and is expecting two further cuts by year-end. Current estimates are placing rate-cut odds for only one further cut in 2026. So all else equal, rates could fall by 100 bps relative to current levels, which would cut out ~25% of Circle's revenue stream.

Note as well that Circle still pays out a large portion of this revenue as "distribution fees" to its large crypto wallet partners, namely Coinbase and Binance. As rates have fallen and as Circle has focused on growth, its RLDC margin (revenue less distribution cost) has also dwindled somewhat to 38%, meaning that Circle only generates 38% of clean net revenue for each dollar it earns in interest income.

While I agree that interest rate declines are a headwind for Circle, there are two reasons that I think the company will still be able to pull ahead:

  • Circulation growth is far outpacing expected interest rate declines. As I previously mentioned, a ~100 bps fall in interest rates will hurt interest revenue by ~25%, relative to the company's current ~4% reserve return rate. But at the same time, USDC circulation grew at 90% y/y in the most recent quarter. I also think that the recent risk-off attitude in the stock market and the selloff in momentum stocks will push more investors to increase their allocation to cash - and USDC is now a core alternative for cash holdings. Coinbase currently pays a 4.1% yield on USDC held on its platform. All in all, Circle is still well poised for revenue growth as stablecoin participation and usage itself grows, and as Circle's own market share expands.
  • Alternative revenue streams. Circle recently released the Arc blockchain, which is its own payment protocol that is natively designed to support USDC transactions (as an alternative to using USDC on other blockchains, such as Ethereum). Circle can earn "gas" fees from transactions on the Arc network, which is a revenue stream that didn't exist entirely in 2024. While it's still early days and we have yet to see how wide of an adoption that Arc can achieve, this initiative helps to boost Circle's horizon beyond earning just interest alone.

Healthy adjusted EBITDA margin expansion and reasonable valuation

We also think that amid top-line concerns, Circle's growing profitability shouldn't be ignored. In Q2, Circle grew adjusted EBITDA at a 52% y/y pace (again, this is in spite of a 103bps y/y decline in reserve return rates) to $126 million, representing a 50% margin against revenue less distribution and transaction costs (the 38% RLDC margin we discussed earlier). This improved 5 points y/y.

Circle is guiding to $475-$490 million in full-year adjusted opex (excluding stock comp), which implies quarterly opex holding stable at ~$125 million per quarter through the balance of the year.

The company has also guided to a 40% CAGR in USDC circulation (which should more than offset interest rate pressure), growth in "other revenue" including the new Arc blockchain initiative, and RLDC margins stabilizing in the 36-38% range. To me, this is a powerful combination for further adjusted EBITDA margin expansion.

At face value, Circle looks expensive at a 58x forward adjusted EBITDA that sits slightly above 2x of Coinbase's valuation.

But the reason I think Circle's valuation is quite reasonable is because of its rapid pace of current adjusted EBITDA expansion (with all the factors necessary for continued growth still in place: USDC circulation growth well in excess of interest rate declines, modest opex growth, and stable distribution costs) which makes near-term adjusted EBITDA multiples less reliable.

Coinbase, on the other hand, looks cheap relative to Circle: but the vast majority of Coinbase's revenue depends on trading revenue, which is very volatile and fluctuates widely from quarter to quarter, whereas Circle's revenue is generated from a very stable interest income stream. In fact, Coinbase's revenue essentially flatlined in the most recent quarter (a function of lower crypto trading volatility) while adjusted EBITDA declined -14% y/y.

Key takeaways

I'd much rather bank on a stable, predictable company like Circle rather than bet on Coinbase's very volatile trading revenue. In my view, the tailwinds from the passage of the GENIUS act this year is unlocking substantial adoption of stablecoins and, in particular, USDC, which is positioning Circle for tremendous revenue growth in spite of interest rate headwinds. Interest rate risk is already priced into the stock: stay long and use the dip as a buying opportunity.
